title: Host.project_root() is never memoized at the process level — every BLOCK/REMIND/WARN re-resolves it, multiplying Codex's per-call subprocess spawn within a single invocation
evidence: _hooklib.get_host() caches the Host OBJECT (module-level `_HOST`, _hooklib.py:76-84), but not the RESULT of calling `host.project_root()` — `_hooklib.project_root()` (line 341-353) calls `get_host().project_root()` fresh on every call. `main()` in each entry script calls it once to compute `root`, but `_log_gate_event` (line 660-685, the shared sink block()/remind()/warn() funnel through — line 681: `root = project_root()`) independently calls it AGAIN every time a gate fires. block(), remind(), and warn() (lines 696-712) each invoke `_log_gate_event` unconditionally, so any hook invocation that hits a REMIND (e.g. the routine H-09/H-10 crypto/secret nudge on a matching write) or WARN (any degraded-path breadcrumb) resolves project_root() a second, independent time in the same process. Under CodexHost (plugins/ca-codex/hooks/_host.py:173-198) there is no project-dir env var and no payload is ever threaded to this second call either, so each of these re-resolutions is its own `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` subprocess spawn — a single Codex hook invocation that both computes root in main() AND fires one REMIND pays TWO subprocess spawns, not one.
impact: On the Codex host, any hook invocation that logs a gate event (REMIND/WARN, which are common — not just rare BLOCKs) pays an extra git subprocess spawn beyond the one already incurred resolving root at the top of main() (performance-001), compounding latency on the synchronous tool-call path. On Claude Code this is cheap today only because CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR is normally set (env lookup, no subprocess) — but the redundant re-resolution itself is present for both hosts and is architecturally unbounded (any future host without a fast env-var path inherits the same multiplication).
recommendation: Memoize the resolved project_root() STRING at the process level (a module-level cache in _hooklib alongside `_HOST`, invalidated only by identity of the process — hooks are single-shot so no invalidation is needed), so main()'s call and any later block()/remind()/warn() call inside the same invocation reuse the first resolution instead of re-invoking Host.project_root().
acceptance_criteria: A hook invocation that calls project_root() and later fires a REMIND/WARN resolves the root exactly once (verifiable by a call-count assertion on Host.project_root or the underlying subprocess.run)
No verdict changes for either host
